Apply Bordeaux paste on the cut ends to prevent fungal infections

stages.title: Basin Preparation & Manuring
substeps: Clean the tree basins and prepare them for irrigation
Apply Farm Yard Manure (FYM), Neem cake, and a basal dose of Phosphorus and Potassium
Apply the first split dose of Nitrogen

stages.title: Spring Flush & Flowering
substeps: New shoots and white fragrant flowers emerge simultaneously
Ensure adequate bee population for pollination
Provide light irrigation to maintain soil moisture without causing waterlogging

stages.title: Fruit Set & Irrigation
substeps: Provide critical irrigation to prevent 'June drop' of young fruits
Apply the second split dose of Nitrogen
Spray plant growth regulators (like 2,4-D or GA3) to minimize fruit drop if recommended

stages.title: Fruit Development & Nutrient Spray
substeps: Apply foliar sprays of micronutrients (Zinc, Iron, Manganese) to prevent chlorosis
Maintain consistent moisture levels through drip irrigation during fruit enlargement
Ensure the orchard is well-drained during the monsoon season

stages.title: Pest & Disease Management
substeps: Monitor rigorously for Citrus Psylla, Leaf Miner, and Whiteflies
Apply systemic insecticides or neem-based sprays as required
Monitor for Citrus Canker and apply copper-based fungicides

stages.title: Color Break & Maturation
substeps: Fruits begin to change color from green to vibrant orange (color break)
Stop Nitrogen application to enhance fruit color and sugar accumulation
Withhold heavy irrigation close to harvest to improve TSS (Total Soluble Solids)

stages.title: Harvesting & Post Harvest
substeps: Harvest fruits using clippers to cut the stem close to the fruit without pulling
Wash, wax, and grade the fruits by size to improve shelf life and market value
Pack carefully in corrugated boxes and store in cool conditions

stages.title: Bahar Treatment (Water Stress & Pruning)
substeps: Withhold irrigation for 3-4 weeks to induce temporary stress and leaf drop (Bahar treatment)
Perform light pruning to remove dead wood and water sprouts
This synchronizes flowering and regulates the fruiting season (Ambe, Mrig, or Hasta Bahar)

stages.title: Basin Preparation & Manuring
substeps: Loosen the soil in the tree basins
Apply a heavy dose of well-rotted FYM, Neem cake, and basal NPK fertilizers
Resume light irrigation to break the stress and stimulate new growth

stages.title: Flowering & New Flush
substeps: A profuse flush of new leaves and fragrant white/purplish flowers appears
Maintain consistent soil moisture to prevent flower drop
Protect the flowers from early pest attacks like aphids

stages.title: Fruit Set & Irrigation
substeps: Provide regular irrigation as the small fruits set and begin to grow
Apply a top dressing of Nitrogen
Spray growth regulators (e.g., 2,4-D) to prevent excessive premature fruit drop

stages.title: Fruit Development & Nutrient Application
substeps: Apply foliar sprays containing Zinc, Manganese, and Iron to prevent leaf yellowing (chlorosis)
Maintain optimal moisture for juice accumulation and fruit enlargement
Mulch the basins to conserve moisture during hot weather

stages.title: Pest & Disease Control
substeps: Monitor rigorously for Citrus Leaf Miner and Citrus Butterfly caterpillars
Check for Citrus Canker (bacterial disease) on leaves and fruits; apply Copper Oxychloride
Manage red spider mites during dry periods

stages.title: Maturation & Color Change
substeps: Fruits mature and transition from dark green to light green or yellow
Reduce irrigation slightly to enhance juice quality and Vitamin C content
Monitor for fruit piercing moths

stages.title: Harvesting & Grading
substeps: Harvest fruits based on market preference (green-mature or fully yellow)
Harvest multiple times as lemons do not mature all at once
Wash, grade by size and color, and pack in ventilated crates for the market

stages.title: Post-Harvest Pruning & Cleaning
substeps: Prune immediately after harvest to remove fruited twigs and encourage new flush
Remove dead, diseased, and interlocking branches to improve aeration
Clean the tree basins and orchard floor of fallen leaves and debris

stages.title: Basin Preparation & Manuring
substeps: Loosen the soil in the tree basins without disturbing the shallow root system
Apply a heavy dose of well-rotted Farm Yard Manure (FYM) and neem cake
Apply basal NPK fertilizers to support the upcoming vegetative flush

stages.title: Winter Dormancy & Moisture Stress
substeps: Withhold irrigation during winter months to induce moisture and temperature stress
Stress is essential to stop vegetative growth and promote floral induction
Monitor the orchard for early signs of Litchi Mite infestation

stages.title: Panicle Emergence & Flowering
substeps: Resume light irrigation as flower panicles begin to emerge in late winter/early spring
Ensure high honeybee activity in the orchard, as litchi requires cross-pollination
Strictly avoid any insecticide sprays during peak flowering

stages.title: Fruit Set & Irrigation
substeps: Increase irrigation frequency after fruit set to prevent early fruit drop
Apply a top dressing of Nitrogen and Potassium
Spray plant growth regulators (like NAA) if heavy fruit drop is observed

stages.title: Fruit Development & Pest Control
substeps: Maintain consistent soil moisture to support rapid aril (pulp) development
Monitor rigorously for the Litchi Fruit Borer
Apply safe, targeted insecticidal sprays or use Trichogramma bio-agents

stages.title: Color Development & Netting
substeps: Fruits turn from green to a distinct pinkish-red color
Cover the tree canopy with anti-bird nets to protect the sweetening fruits from birds and bats
Spray Borax to prevent fruit cracking, a common issue during dry, hot winds

stages.title: Harvesting & Packaging
substeps: Harvest fruits in bunches along with a few leaves to prolong freshness
Pick early in the morning to avoid heat accumulation in the fruits
Pre-cool the litchis immediately, pack in ventilated boxes, and dispatch quickly due to high perishability

stages.title: Pruning & Canopy Management
substeps: Perform structural pruning post-harvest to open the center of the canopy
Remove dead wood, water sprouts, and branches affected by mango malformation
Apply copper fungicide paste on thick cut ends

stages.title: Basin Preparation & Manuring
substeps: Prepare ring basins around the tree trunk, keeping the area near the trunk dry
Apply heavy organic manure (FYM) and a basal dose of Phosphorus and Potassium
Apply Paclobutrazol (Cultar) in the soil for regular bearing in irregular varieties

stages.title: Vegetative Flush & Pest Control
substeps: New copper-colored leaves (flush) emerge and gradually turn green
Monitor for Mango Hopper and Leaf Webber on the tender new leaves
Apply appropriate insecticidal sprays to protect the vegetative growth

stages.title: Winter Dormancy & Flower Initiation
substeps: Withhold irrigation to induce stress, which stops vegetative growth
Cool winter temperatures naturally trigger floral bud differentiation
Keep the orchard floor clean of weeds to slightly raise ambient soil temperature

stages.title: Panicle Emergence & Pollination
substeps: Flower panicles emerge; apply a prophylactic spray against Powdery Mildew
Ensure presence of houseflies and blowflies, which are the primary pollinators for mangoes
Do not irrigate during full bloom to prevent flower drop

stages.title: Fruit Set (Pea/Marble Stage) & Irrigation
substeps: Resume irrigation once fruits reach the size of a pea/marble
Apply a top dressing of Nitrogen and Potassium
Spray NAA (Naphthaleneacetic acid) to minimize natural fruit drop

stages.title: Fruit Bulking & Bagging
substeps: Maintain regular irrigation (every 10-15 days) to ensure optimal fruit sizing
Bag premium fruits on the tree to protect from fruit flies and improve skin color
Monitor for Mango Fruit Fly and set up methyl eugenol pheromone traps

stages.title: Harvesting & Post Harvest
substeps: Harvest fruits when the shoulders outgrow the stem end and skin color lightens
Use a mango harvester with a net to pick fruits with a 1-2 cm pedicel attached to prevent sap burn
Wash, desap, grade, and treat with hot water before packing for ripening

stages.title: Bahar Treatment & Pruning
substeps: Withhold water for 3-4 weeks (typically Ambe Bahar in winter) to induce leaf drop and stress
Prune dead, diseased, and crossing branches to open up the canopy
Apply Bordeaux paste to cut branches to prevent fungal entry

stages.title: Basin Preparation & Manuring
substeps: Loosen the soil in the tree basins without injuring feeding roots
Apply a heavy dose of FYM, Neem cake, and basal NPK fertilizers
Resume light irrigation to break the stress and stimulate budding

stages.title: Flowering & Flush
substeps: A profuse flush of new leaves and white flowers emerges
Maintain consistent soil moisture to prevent blossom drop
Protect the delicate flowers from early pest attacks like aphids

stages.title: Fruit Set & Irrigation
substeps: Provide regular irrigation as the small fruits set and begin to grow
Apply a top dressing of Nitrogen
Spray growth regulators to prevent excessive premature fruit drop

stages.title: Fruit Development & Nutrient Spray
substeps: Apply foliar sprays containing Zinc, Manganese, and Iron to prevent leaf yellowing (chlorosis)
Maintain optimal moisture for juice accumulation and fruit enlargement
Mulch the basins to conserve moisture during hot weather

stages.title: Pest & Disease Control
substeps: Monitor rigorously for Citrus Leaf Miner and Citrus Psylla
Check for Citrus Canker on leaves and fruits; apply Copper Oxychloride
Manage red spider mites during dry periods

stages.title: Maturation & Color Change
substeps: Fruits mature and transition from dark green to a lighter yellowish-green
Reduce irrigation slightly to enhance juice sweetness and quality
Monitor the orchard for fruit piercing moths

stages.title: Harvesting & Grading
substeps: Harvest fruits when the skin becomes tight, glossy, and light green/yellow
Clip fruits carefully from the stem to avoid rind damage
Wash, grade by size, and pack in ventilated crates for the fresh juice market

stages.title: Pruning & Dead Wood Removal
substeps: Prune the tree to remove dead, diseased, and non-productive wood
Remove water sprouts and interlocking branches to ensure proper aeration
Apply Bordeaux paste on the cut ends to prevent fungal infections

stages.title: Basin Preparation & Manuring
substeps: Clean the tree basins and prepare them for efficient irrigation
Apply Farm Yard Manure (FYM), Neem cake, and a basal dose of Phosphorus and Potassium
Apply the first split dose of Nitrogen

stages.title: Spring Flush & Flowering
substeps: New shoots and fragrant white flowers emerge simultaneously
Ensure adequate bee population for pollination
Provide light irrigation to maintain soil moisture without causing waterlogging

stages.title: Fruit Set & Irrigation
substeps: Provide critical irrigation to prevent the early drop of young fruits
Apply the second split dose of Nitrogen
Spray plant growth regulators to minimize fruit drop if environmental stress is high

stages.title: Fruit Development & Nutrient Spray
substeps: Apply foliar sprays of micronutrients (Zinc, Iron, Manganese) to prevent citrus decline
Maintain consistent moisture levels through drip irrigation during fruit enlargement
Ensure the orchard is well-drained during the monsoon season

stages.title: Pest & Disease Management
substeps: Monitor rigorously for Citrus Psylla, Leaf Miner, and Whiteflies
Apply systemic insecticides or neem-based sprays as required
Monitor for Phytophthora root rot and apply appropriate fungicides

stages.title: Color Break & Maturation
substeps: Fruits begin to change color from green to vibrant orange (color break)
Stop Nitrogen application to enhance fruit color and sugar accumulation
Withhold heavy irrigation close to harvest to improve Total Soluble Solids (TSS)

stages.title: Harvesting & Post Harvest
substeps: Harvest fruits using clippers to cut the stem close to the fruit without pulling
Wash, wax, and grade the fruits by size to improve shelf life and market value
Pack carefully in corrugated boxes and store in cool, ventilated conditions

stages.title: Nursery Preparation
substeps: Sow seeds in raised beds or polythene bags filled with a mix of soil, sand, and FYM
Treat seeds with Captan or Thiram to prevent damping-off disease
Protect the nursery from heavy rain and direct, harsh sunlight

stages.title: Land Prep & Transplanting
substeps: Deep plough the main field and prepare pits of 45x45x45 cm at 1.8x1.8m spacing
Transplant vigorous 30-day-old seedlings (plant 2-3 per pit if using dioecious varieties)
Irrigate immediately; ensure absolute prevention of waterlogging as papaya roots rot quickly

stages.title: Vegetative Growth & Weeding
substeps: Keep the field completely weed-free through shallow manual hoeing
Apply top dressing of Nitrogen and Potassium in split doses
Monitor for early signs of Papaya Ring Spot Virus (PRSV) and uproot infected plants

stages.title: Fertilizer Application & Earthing Up
substeps: Papaya is a heavy feeder; apply fertilizers every 2 months
Apply micronutrients like Boron and Zinc to prevent bumpy fruits
Earth up the soil around the stem base to provide support against strong winds

stages.title: Flowering & Sex Identification
substeps: Flowers appear; identify the sex of the plants in dioecious varieties
Remove excess male plants, retaining only 10% scattered across the field for pollination
Retain only one vigorous female or hermaphrodite plant per pit

stages.title: Fruit Set & Irrigation
substeps: Maintain consistent soil moisture using drip irrigation for optimal fruit set
Moisture stress causes flower drop and misshapen fruits
Thin out crowded fruits on the trunk to allow remaining fruits to size up properly

stages.title: Fruit Development & Pest Control
substeps: Monitor rigorously for Mealybugs and Whiteflies (virus vectors)
Apply neem oil or appropriate systemic insecticides
Support heavily fruiting trunks with bamboo poles to prevent snapping

stages.title: Harvesting & Post Harvest
substeps: Harvest fruits when they show a tinge of yellow at the apical end (color break)
Twist the fruit gently or cut the stalk to avoid bruising the delicate skin
Wrap individual fruits in paper, pack in single-layer boxes, and ripen or dispatch

stages.title: Winter Dormancy & Pruning
substeps: Perform dormant pruning to maintain an open-center canopy
Remove weak, diseased, and crossing branches
Apply dormant oil and copper sprays to control leaf curl and overwintering pests

stages.title: Basin Preparation & Manuring
substeps: Clean the tree basins and remove weeds
Apply well-rotted Farm Yard Manure (FYM) and a basal dose of NPK
Ensure the orchard floor is clean to reduce frost risk

stages.title: Bud Swell & Flowering
substeps: Pink buds emerge followed by full bloom
Protect blossoms from late spring frost using heaters or wind machines
Ensure adequate bee activity for pollination, though many peach varieties are self-fertile

stages.title: Fruit Set & Thinning
substeps: Wait for natural 'June drop' to occur
Perform manual fruit thinning to leave 1 fruit every 15-20 cm on the branch
Thinning is critical to achieve marketable fruit size and prevent branch breakage

stages.title: Fruit Development & Irrigation
substeps: Provide regular and deep irrigation during the rapid fruit swelling phase
Apply a top dressing of Potassium to improve fruit size and sugar content
Monitor rigorously for the Oriental Fruit Moth and Peach Twig Borer

stages.title: Color Development & Maturation
substeps: Fruits undergo the final swell and background color changes from green to yellow/creamy white
Reduce irrigation slightly to concentrate sugars and improve flavor
Monitor for Brown Rot and apply protective fungicides if humidity is high

stages.title: Harvesting
substeps: Harvest when the fruit is firm but the background color has fully transitioned
Pick fruits delicately using the palm of the hand to avoid finger bruising
Conduct multiple pickings every 2-3 days as fruits ripen unevenly

stages.title: Post-Harvest Maintenance
substeps: Apply post-harvest irrigation and a light dose of Nitrogen to help the tree recover
Sort, grade, and pre-cool harvested peaches immediately to halt ripening
Pack in soft, molded trays for transport to the market

stages.title: Winter Dormancy & Pruning
substeps: Prune trees to maintain a central leader or modified leader system
Remove vertical water sprouts and retain horizontal fruiting spurs
Apply dormant sprays to control scale insects and mite eggs

stages.title: Basin Preparation & Fertilizer
substeps: Apply heavy organic manures and basal NPK to the tree basins
Test soil pH; pears prefer slightly acidic to neutral soils
Repair drip irrigation lines before the active growing season begins

stages.title: Bud Break & Flowering
substeps: White flower clusters emerge along with the new leaves
Introduce honeybee hives, as pears require cross-pollination from a compatible variety
Apply prophylactic sprays containing Streptomycin if Fire Blight is a known risk in the area

stages.title: Fruit Set & Early Growth
substeps: Monitor fruit set and perform light thinning if the cluster is too dense
Apply foliar sprays of Boron and Calcium to improve fruit set and quality
Provide steady irrigation to support early cellular division in the fruit

stages.title: Fruit Development & Irrigation
substeps: Maintain uniform soil moisture; pears are sensitive to drought stress
Monitor for Codling Moth and Pear Psylla; apply targeted insecticidal sprays
Apply summer pruning to remove excessive shading and improve airflow

stages.title: Maturation & Sugar Accumulation
substeps: Fruits increase in size and firmness
Check fruit maturity using a penetrometer (testing fruit firmness)
Observe a slight change in the skin's background color from dark green to a lighter yellowish-green

stages.title: Harvesting
substeps: Harvest European pears while they are still hard and mature-green (they ripen off the tree)
Harvest Asian pears when they are fully ripe and crisp on the tree
Lift the fruit gently and twist upwards to keep the stem attached without breaking the spur

stages.title: Post-Harvest & Storage
substeps: Cool European pears immediately and store them in cold rooms (0-1°C) to initiate the ripening process
Sort and grade fruits, removing any with skin blemishes or codling moth damage
Apply post-harvest orchard maintenance, including a light fertilization and leaf fall cleanup

stages.title: Land Preparation & Trenching
substeps: Perform deep ploughing and harrowing to achieve a fine tilth
Prepare trenches or raised beds with 90 cm spacing between beds
Incorporate heavy doses of FYM and ensure excellent drainage to prevent heart rot

stages.title: Sucker Selection & Planting
substeps: Select uniform, healthy slips or suckers (350-450g) for planting
Cure the suckers in the shade for a few days and strip lower leaves
Dip the base in fungicide and plant in double rows at 25x60 cm spacing

stages.title: Vegetative Growth & Weeding
substeps: Apply pre-emergence herbicides (e.g., Diuron) as pineapples are poor weed competitors
Perform regular manual weeding between the plants
Provide light, frequent irrigation during dry spells

stages.title: Fertilizer Application & Earthing Up
substeps: Apply Nitrogen and Potassium in split doses (pineapple requires high Potassium)
Perform earthing up to prevent plants from lodging (falling over) as they grow heavy
Monitor for Mealybugs and treat the soil/plants if detected

stages.title: Flower Induction
substeps: Apply flower-inducing chemicals (Ethrel/Ethephon mixed with Urea and Sodium Carbonate)
Pour the solution into the heart (center) of the mature plant (usually at 35-40 leaf stage)
This ensures uniform flowering and synchronized harvesting

stages.title: Flowering & Fruit Set
substeps: The red inflorescence emerges from the center of the plant 45-60 days after induction
The individual purple flowers open sequentially from bottom to top
Protect the field from extreme heat and ensure consistent moisture

stages.title: Fruit Development & Bulking
substeps: The multiple flowers fuse to form the compound fruit (syncarp)
Apply a final foliar spray of Potassium Nitrate to increase fruit size and TSS
Cover the fruits with dry grass or paper to prevent severe sunburn during summer

stages.title: Harvesting & Post Harvest
substeps: Harvest for local markets when 25% of the fruit base turns yellow-orange
Harvest for canning or distant markets when the color break just begins at the base
Cut the fruit retaining a small portion of the stalk, grade by weight, and pack securely

stages.title: Winter Pruning & Dormancy
substeps: Prune trees to an open-center or vase shape to maximize sunlight penetration
Remove water sprouts, dead wood, and older, less productive spurs
Apply dormant sprays (copper-based) to control shot-hole disease and overwintering pests

stages.title: Manuring & Basin Prep
substeps: Apply well-decomposed FYM, Phosphorus, and Potassium in the tree basins
Loosen the soil and repair irrigation channels
Apply a split dose of Nitrogen just before bud break

stages.title: Flowering & Pollination
substeps: Plums bloom profusely with white flowers before or alongside leaf emergence
Japanese plums generally require a pollinator variety; ensure bees are active
Protect orchards from late spring frosts

stages.title: Fruit Set & Thinning
substeps: Heavy fruit set is common; manual or chemical thinning is essential
Thin fruits to be spaced 5-10 cm apart to ensure good size and prevent branch breakage
Apply a second dose of Nitrogen to support early growth

stages.title: Fruit Development & Irrigation
substeps: Provide regular irrigation; drought stress can cause severe fruit drop and poor sizing
Monitor rigorously for the Plum Curculio, aphids, and fruit flies
Apply fungicides if conditions are wet to prevent Brown Rot

stages.title: Color Break & Maturation
substeps: Fruits undergo a final rapid swell and change color (red, purple, or yellow depending on variety)
A natural waxy coating (bloom) develops on the skin
Withhold heavy irrigation close to harvest to prevent fruit cracking and improve sweetness

stages.title: Harvesting
substeps: Harvest fruits when they yield slightly to pressure and have full color
Pick plums with the stem attached and handle carefully to preserve the waxy 'bloom'
Conduct 2-3 pickings as the fruits ripen sequentially

stages.title: Post-Harvest Care
substeps: Cool the fruits immediately after harvest and store at 0°C for extended shelf life
Provide post-harvest irrigation to the orchard to maintain leaf health until autumn
Clean the orchard floor of fallen, mummified fruits to reduce disease pressure for the next year

stages.title: Bahar Treatment & Stress
substeps: Withhold irrigation for 30-40 days to induce stress and leaf drop (Ambe, Mrig, or Hasta Bahar)
This synchronizes flowering and regulates the harvest timing to avoid severe market gluts
Lightly plow the orchard to expose roots and control soil pests

stages.title: Pruning & Basal Manuring
substeps: Prune the bushes to maintain a multi-stem training system (3-4 main stems)
Remove water sprouts, dead wood, and crossing branches to keep the center open
Apply heavy doses of FYM, Neem cake, and NPK fertilizers in the basins, then resume irrigation

stages.title: Sprouting & Flowering
substeps: New reddish-green foliage appears followed closely by bell-shaped red/orange flowers
Male, female, and hermaphrodite flowers emerge; only hermaphrodite flowers set fruit
Ensure adequate soil moisture to prevent flower drop

stages.title: Fruit Set & Irrigation
substeps: Fruit set begins; perform light manual thinning if clusters are too dense (retain 1-2 fruits per cluster)
Maintain uniform soil moisture using drip irrigation
Apply a top dressing of Nitrogen and Potassium

stages.title: Fruit Development & Aril Formation
substeps: Fruits rapidly increase in size; the juicy arils inside develop their color and sweetness
Apply foliar sprays of Boron and Calcium to strengthen the rind and prevent fruit cracking
Monitor rigorously for the Anar Butterfly (Fruit Borer) and apply targeted sprays or bag the fruits

stages.title: Disease Control & Sunburn Protection
substeps: Monitor strictly for Bacterial Blight (Telya disease) – oily spots on leaves and fruits
Apply Streptocycline and Copper-based sprays to manage bacterial blight
Provide light shading or spray Kaolin clay to protect exposed fruits from severe sunburn

stages.title: Maturation & Color Change
substeps: The rind color changes to a deep red or yellowish-red depending on the variety (e.g., Bhagwa)
The calyx at the bottom of the fruit folds inward
Stop irrigation a week before harvest to increase TSS (sugar content) and shelf life

stages.title: Harvesting & Post Harvest
substeps: Harvest fruits using secateurs, cutting the stem close to the fruit to avoid puncturing others
Harvesting is done in 3-4 pickings as fruits mature
Wash, sort, grade by size and weight, and pack in corrugated boxes with paper shreds for transport

stages.title: Land Preparation & Pit Digging
substeps: Plough the land 2-3 times to a fine tilth
Prepare pits of 60x60x60 cm or long furrows at 2x2 meter spacing
Incorporate well-rotted FYM and neem cake into the pits

stages.title: Seed Sowing
substeps: Soak seeds in water for 12 hours prior to sowing
Treat seeds with Trichoderma viride to prevent soil-borne diseases
Sow 2-3 seeds per pit at a depth of 2-3 cm

stages.title: Vine Growth & Thinning
substeps: Thin out weak seedlings, leaving the best 1-2 plants per pit
Perform shallow hand weeding to avoid damaging the shallow root system
Train the vines to spread evenly across the field

stages.title: Fertilizer Application & Irrigation
substeps: Apply a top dressing of Nitrogen to boost vine growth
Irrigate the field every 5-7 days depending on soil moisture
Ensure adequate Potassium supply for good fruit size and sweetness

stages.title: Flowering & Pollination
substeps: Male and female flowers appear on the vines
Ensure healthy honeybee activity for effective cross-pollination
Avoid spraying chemical pesticides during morning hours to protect bees

stages.title: Fruit Development
substeps: Place dry straw under developing fruits to prevent soil-borne fruit rot
Maintain consistent moisture to prevent fruit cracking
Stop heavy irrigation 3-5 days before harvest to concentrate sugars

stages.title: Harvesting
substeps: Check for a dull, hollow sound when the fruit is tapped with knuckles
Observe the ground spot turning from white to pale yellow
Cut the fruit from the vine leaving a 2-inch stalk attached

stages.title: Summer Irrigation & Phytosanitation
substeps: Provide life-saving sprinkler or drip irrigation during hot summer months
Clean the palm crowns by removing dead leaves and dried spathes
Protect stems from sunscorch by tying dried leaves around the trunk

stages.title: Pre-Monsoon Manuring
substeps: Open circular trenches around the base of the palms
Apply green leaf manure, heavy FYM, and basal NPK fertilizers
Cover the trenches with soil to prevent nutrient runoff during rains

stages.title: Inflorescence Emergence & Fruit Set
substeps: The spathe opens to reveal the inflorescence
Cross-pollination occurs primarily via wind and insects
Young button nuts set and begin early expansion

stages.title: Monsoon Maintenance & Disease Control
substeps: Ensure proper drainage in the plantation to prevent waterlogging
Spray 1% Bordeaux mixture strictly before and during the monsoon to prevent Koleroga (Fruit Rot)
Cover developing bunches with polythene covers in high-rainfall areas

stages.title: Nut Development & Bulking
substeps: Nuts undergo rapid expansion and the inner kernel solidifies
The outer husk fibrous tissue develops
Monitor for spindle bug and root grub attacks; apply necessary controls

stages.title: Harvesting
substeps: For Kalipak (boiled nut), harvest when nuts are three-quarters ripe and green
For Chali (white supari), harvest when nuts are fully ripe and orange-yellow
Skilled climbers harvest the bunches and lower them carefully

stages.title: Post Harvest Processing
substeps: Dehusk the harvested nuts manually or mechanically
For Kalipak: Boil the tender kernels in water with previous year's extract, then sun-dry
For Chali: Sun-dry the whole ripe nuts for 40-45 days before dehusking

stages.title: Summer Management & Shade Regulation
substeps: Prune the branches of shade trees (standards) to allow optimal sunlight penetration
Mulch the base of the pepper vines with dry leaves to conserve moisture
Provide protective irrigation if summer drought is severe

stages.title: Pre-Monsoon Manuring & Trailing
substeps: Tie the growing pepper vines to the standard trees using natural fibers
Apply organic compost, neem cake, and basal NPK around the base of the vines
Avoid deep digging around the base to prevent damaging the shallow root system

stages.title: Spike Emergence & Flowering
substeps: Flowering is triggered by the first early monsoon showers
Small, catkin-like floral spikes emerge from the nodes
Rainwater is essential for the natural pollination of pepper flowers

stages.title: Monsoon Maintenance & Disease Control
substeps: Monitor rigorously for Phytophthora foot rot (Quick Wilt)
Apply 1% Bordeaux mixture to the vines and drench the soil with Copper Oxychloride
Perform slashing of weeds to prevent high humidity at the ground level

stages.title: Berry Development & Maturation
substeps: The fertilized flowers develop into green berries (drupes) along the spike
Berries undergo a prolonged period of bulking and hardening
Monitor for Pollu beetle damage; apply Neem-based insecticides if needed

stages.title: Harvesting
substeps: Harvesting begins when 1 or 2 berries on a spike turn bright orange/red
Use ladders to hand-pick the mature spikes carefully without damaging the vine
Collect the spikes in clean bags or baskets

stages.title: Post Harvest Processing
substeps: Thresh the spikes to separate the berries manually or using mechanical threshers
Blanch the green berries in boiling water for 1 minute to ensure uniform black color
Sun-dry the berries on clean mats for 3-5 days until moisture drops to 10-11%

stages.title: Summer Irrigation & Shade Management
substeps: Cardamom is highly sensitive to drought; provide regular sprinkler irrigation
Maintain 50-60% filtered shade by regulating the canopy of shade trees
Protect the delicate clumps from scorching direct sunlight

stages.title: Panicle Emergence & Trashing
substeps: Perform 'trashing' by removing dried leaves, old tillers, and weed growth from the clumps
This exposes the base for new panicles (flower stalks) to emerge horizontally
Maintain high humidity around the plant base

stages.title: Pre-Monsoon Manuring
substeps: Apply heavy organic manures (FYM, compost) and neem cake
Apply the first split of NPK fertilizers in a circular band around the clump
Mulch the base heavily to prevent soil erosion during the monsoon

stages.title: Flowering & Pollination
substeps: Flowers open sequentially on the panicles over a long period
Maintain at least 4 healthy honeybee colonies per hectare; cross-pollination is mandatory
Strictly avoid chemical sprays during peak flowering hours

stages.title: Capsule Development & Pest Control
substeps: The pollinated flowers develop into green capsules
Monitor rigorously for Cardamom Thrips, which cause 'scab' on the capsules
Apply targeted, safe insecticides like Quinalphos if pest thresholds are crossed

stages.title: Harvesting
substeps: Capsules take 90-120 days from flowering to reach maturity
Harvesting is done in 5-6 pickings at 20-30 day intervals
Hand-pick only the mature, bold, dark green capsules, leaving immature ones on the panicle

stages.title: Curing & Processing
substeps: Wash the harvested capsules to remove dirt
Cure immediately in specialized curing chambers (kilns) using hot air (45-50°C) for 18-24 hours
Proper curing retains the premium 'parrot green' color and essential oils

stages.title: Post-Harvest Cleaning & Pruning
substeps: Prune the canopy to remove dead, diseased, and intertwining branches
Clear the orchard floor of dried leaves to prevent accidental fires in summer
Prepare semi-circular terraces or basins around the trees on sloping lands

stages.title: Manuring & First TMB Spray
substeps: Apply NPK fertilizers and organic manure in circular trenches around the dripline
Apply the first prophylactic spray to control the Tea Mosquito Bug (TMB)
TMB is the most destructive pest, damaging tender flushes and floral shoots

stages.title: Vegetative Flush & Flowering
substeps: The tree puts forth a new flush of bronze/pink leaves followed by terminal flower panicles
Apply the second TMB spray exactly at the panicle emergence stage
Cashew produces both male and hermaphrodite flowers on the same panicle

stages.title: Fruit Set & Nut Development
substeps: After pollination, the true fruit (the cashew nut) develops rapidly and reaches full size first
Apply the third TMB spray at the early fruit set stage
Monitor for the Cashew Stem and Root Borer; apply mechanical removal or chemical swabbing

stages.title: Cashew Apple Development
substeps: Once the nut reaches full size, the pedicel (stalk) begins to swell rapidly
This swollen stalk forms the fleshy 'cashew apple', which turns red or yellow
The nut shell hardens and turns greyish-brown

stages.title: Harvesting
substeps: The mature cashew apples fall to the ground naturally
Collect the fallen nuts daily to prevent fungal infections and quality deterioration
Alternatively, pick fully ripe apples from the tree if the apple is also being utilized

stages.title: Post Harvest Processing
substeps: Separate the nut from the cashew apple immediately
Sun-dry the raw nuts for 2-3 days until the moisture drops below 9%
Store in gunny bags before sending for commercial processing (roasting, shelling, peeling, grading)

stages.title: Land Preparation
substeps: Deep summer ploughing to break hardpan and destroy pest pupae
Harrow 2-3 times to achieve a moderate tilth
Prepare ridges and furrows at a spacing of 90-120 cm

stages.title: Seed Treatment & Sowing
substeps: Treat seeds with Trichoderma viride or Carbendazim to prevent root rot
Soak seeds in water for 12 hours before sowing for better germination
Dibble seeds on the ridges at a spacing of 45-60 cm

stages.title: Weeding & Thinning
substeps: Apply pre-emergence herbicide like Pendimethalin
Thin out weak seedlings 15-20 days after sowing, leaving one healthy plant per hill
Perform first manual weeding or inter-cultivation using a blade harrow

stages.title: Fertilizer & Irrigation
substeps: Apply basal dose of NPK along with Sulphur (crucial for oilseed crops)
Top dress with Nitrogen at 30-40 days after sowing
Provide life-saving irrigation if monsoon fails; castor is highly drought-tolerant but responds well to watering

stages.title: Spike Emergence & Flowering
substeps: Primary spikes emerge, bearing both male and female flowers
Ensure adequate soil moisture to prevent drying of flowers
Monitor for Castor Semilooper and Spodoptera (tobacco caterpillar); apply neem or chemical insecticides

stages.title: Capsule Development & Pest Control
substeps: Fertilized flowers develop into spiny capsules containing the seeds
Monitor rigorously for the Capsule Borer
Apply second top dressing of Nitrogen to support secondary and tertiary spike development

stages.title: Harvesting
substeps: Harvesting is done in 2-3 pickings as spikes mature unevenly
Pick the spikes when 80% of the capsules turn yellowish-brown and start drying
Cut the spikes with a sickle before the capsules shatter (split open) in the field

stages.title: Post Harvest Drying & Threshing
substeps: Sun dry the harvested spikes on a threshing floor for 4-5 days until they blacken
Beat the dried spikes with wooden sticks or run a tractor over them to separate seeds
Winnow, clean, and store the seeds in dry gunny bags

stages.title: Nursery Preparation & Sowing
substeps: Prepare raised nursery beds enriched with fine compost
Treat seeds with Captan or Thiram to prevent damping-off
Sow seeds in lines, cover with mulch, and provide light daily watering until germination

stages.title: Land Prep & Transplanting
substeps: Plough the main field 3-4 times and apply a heavy dose of FYM
Prepare ridges and furrows at 60 cm spacing
Transplant robust 30-40 day old seedlings in the late afternoon and irrigate immediately

stages.title: Weeding & Fertilizer
substeps: Apply pre-emergence weedicide before transplanting
Perform hand weeding 20 days after transplanting
Apply basal NPK and top dress with Nitrogen at 30 days

stages.title: Vegetative Growth & Branching
substeps: Maintain consistent soil moisture; avoid waterlogging to prevent root rot
Apply micronutrients (Zinc and Boron) to encourage healthy branching
Monitor for thrips, aphids, and mites causing leaf curl

stages.title: Flowering & Fruit Set
substeps: White flowers emerge at the nodes
Spray Planofix (NAA) to prevent severe flower drop caused by heat or moisture stress
Ensure adequate bee activity and maintain soil moisture

stages.title: Maturation & Reddening
substeps: Green chilies mature and begin to turn bright red
Apply Potassium to enhance the red color and capsaicin (pungency) content
Monitor for Fruit Borer (Helicoverpa armigera) and Anthracnose (fruit rot)

stages.title: Harvesting (Pickings)
substeps: Harvest fully ripe, dark red, and slightly shriveled fruits for dry red chili
Conduct multiple pickings every 10-15 days as fruits ripen
Leave the stalk attached during picking to improve shelf life and market value

stages.title: Post Harvest Drying
substeps: Spread the harvested red chilies in a thin layer on clean concrete floors or tarpaulins
Sun dry for 7-10 days, turning them frequently for uniform drying
Dry until moisture reaches 10-12% and chilies become crisp, then pack in gunny bags

stages.title: Post Harvest Maintenance & Pruning
substeps: Perform light pruning to remove dead, diseased, and parasitic branches (Loranthus)
Clean the tree basins of fallen leaves and weed growth
Provide protective irrigation during the dry summer months

stages.title: Basin Prep & Manuring
substeps: Open circular shallow trenches around the dripline of the tree
Apply heavy organic manure (FYM, compost) and bone meal
Apply basal NPK fertilizers before the onset of the monsoon

stages.title: Vegetative Flush & Canopy Management
substeps: The tree produces a new flush of pinkish-green leaves
Ensure adequate drainage as clove trees are highly sensitive to waterlogging
Monitor for stem borers and apply prophylactic treatments to the trunk

stages.title: Bud Emergence (Pre-flowering)
substeps: Terminal floral shoots emerge bearing clusters of tiny green buds
Maintain optimal soil moisture; moisture stress can lead to bud drop
Apply a foliar spray of Potassium to support bud development

stages.title: Bud Swelling & Maturation
substeps: The flower buds swell and change color from pale green to a distinct pinkish-red
Protect the canopy from strong winds which can damage the delicate terminal buds
Prepare harvesting equipment and clean drying mats

stages.title: Harvesting (Unopened Buds)
substeps: Harvest strictly before the blossoms open; open flowers yield inferior spice
Skilled climbers or workers using ladders hand-pick the clusters carefully
Harvesting is done in multiple rounds as buds mature unevenly across the canopy

stages.title: Post Harvest Drying & Processing
substeps: Separate individual buds from the peduncles (stems) manually
Spread the buds evenly on clean mats and sun dry for 4-5 days
Buds turn dark brown and snap cleanly when fully dried (8-10% moisture)

stages.title: Off-season Irrigation & Protection
substeps: Grade the dried cloves and pack in moisture-proof gunny bags
Mulch the tree basins thickly with dried leaves to conserve soil moisture
Resume protective drip or basin irrigation for the next cycle

stages.title: Post-Harvest Pruning & Shade Regulation
substeps: Prune bushes to remove exhausted wood, dead branches, and unwanted suckers (centering)
Regulate the overhead shade canopy (lopping) to allow 50-60% filtered sunlight
Control weeds using manual slashing or contact herbicides

stages.title: Pre-Monsoon Manuring & Blossom Showers
substeps: Apply the first dose of NPK fertilizers in a semi-circle around the drip circle
Provide artificial sprinkler irrigation ('blossom showers') if natural spring rains fail
Adequate moisture is critical to force the dormant flower buds to open

stages.title: Flowering (Blossom) & Pollination
substeps: White, jasmine-scented flowers bloom profusely 7-10 days after the blossom showers
Ensure high honeybee activity; Arabica is self-pollinating, but Robusta requires cross-pollination
Provide 'backing showers' (irrigation) 2-3 weeks later to ensure successful fruit set

stages.title: Pinhead Stage & Berry Set
substeps: Fertilized flowers drop their petals, leaving tiny green 'pinhead' berries
Apply prophylactic sprays of Bordeaux mixture to prevent Leaf Rust (Hemileia vastatrix)
Apply a second round of fertilization focusing on Nitrogen and Potassium

stages.title: Monsoon Management & Berry Expansion
substeps: Berries expand rapidly during the heavy monsoon rains
Ensure excellent trench drainage to prevent waterlogging and root rot
Monitor rigorously for Coffee Berry Borer (CBB) and White Stem Borer; apply traps/sprays

stages.title: Maturation & Color Change
substeps: Green berries harden and slowly begin transitioning to a yellowish-green
As monsoon ends, regulate shade again to expose berries to more sunlight for ripening
Apply the final dose of post-monsoon fertilizers

stages.title: Harvesting (Fly picking & Main picking)
substeps: Berries turn bright red ('cherry' stage)
Conduct 'fly picking' to harvest early-ripening berries
Conduct main picking, selectively hand-harvesting only the fully red cherries (crucial for quality)

stages.title: Post-Harvest Processing
substeps: For Washed Coffee (Parchment): Pulp the red cherries within 12 hours, ferment, wash, and sun-dry
For Natural Coffee (Cherry): Sun-dry the whole red cherries directly on patios for 15-20 days
Store the dried parchment/cherry in well-ventilated godowns

stages.title: Land Preparation
substeps: Plough the land 2-3 times to achieve a fine, friable tilth
Remove all stubble and weeds from previous crops
Prepare flat beds or ridges depending on the irrigation method

stages.title: Seed Treatment & Sowing
substeps: Gently trample or rub the seeds (schizocarps) to split them into two halves
Treat seeds with Trichoderma viride to prevent wilt and root rot
Line sow at a depth of 1-1.5 cm with a row spacing of 30 cm

stages.title: Germination & Early Growth
substeps: Provide light irrigation immediately after sowing
Seeds take 10-15 days to germinate; maintain consistent surface moisture
Protect emerging seedlings from bird damage

stages.title: Thinning, Weeding & Leaf Harvest
substeps: Thin out crowded seedlings to maintain a plant-to-plant spacing of 10-15 cm
Perform shallow hand weeding to keep the field weed-free
If grown for greens, partial leaf harvesting can begin at 30-40 days

stages.title: Fertilizer Application & Irrigation
substeps: Apply basal dose of NPK; avoid excessive Nitrogen which promotes vegetative growth over seed yield
Top dress with a light dose of Urea
Irrigate at 10-12 day intervals depending on soil moisture

stages.title: Flowering (Umbell Emergence)
substeps: Plants transition to the reproductive phase and white/pinkish umbels (flowers) emerge
Monitor rigorously for aphids on the tender flower stalks
Apply safe botanical sprays (Neem) to protect pollinators

stages.title: Seed Maturation
substeps: Flowers develop into green seeds which gradually turn yellowish-brown
Monitor for powdery mildew and apply wettable sulphur if necessary
Stop irrigation as the crop approaches maturity to aid drying

stages.title: Harvesting & Threshing
substeps: Harvest when 50-60% of the seeds on the umbel turn brown (avoids shattering)
Cut or uproot the plants in the early morning while dew is present to minimize seed drop
Sun dry the bundles for 2-3 days, beat gently with sticks to thresh, winnow, and store

stages.title: Land Preparation
substeps: Perform deep summer ploughing to expose soil-borne pests
Harrow 2-3 times to achieve a fine tilth
Apply well-decomposed Farm Yard Manure (FYM) uniformly

stages.title: Seed Treatment & Sowing
substeps: Perform acid delinting of seeds to remove fuzz
Treat seeds with Imidacloprid and Trichoderma
Dibble seeds on ridges or flat beds with 90x60 cm spacing

stages.title: Vegetative Growth & Weeding
substeps: Perform gap filling within 10 days if germination is poor
Apply pre-emergence herbicide like Pendimethalin
Perform hand weeding and earth up the plants at 30 days

stages.title: Fertilizer Application & Irrigation
substeps: Apply basal dose of Phosphorus and Potassium
Apply Nitrogen in 3-4 split doses during vegetative and square formation stages
Irrigate using drip system; strictly avoid waterlogging

stages.title: Square Formation & Flowering
substeps: Monitor rigorously for sucking pests (Whiteflies, Jassids, Aphids)
Spray Planofix (NAA) to prevent excessive square and flower drop
Apply Potassium Nitrate spray to support boll development

stages.title: Boll Formation & Pest Control
substeps: Set up pheromone traps for Pink Bollworm monitoring
Apply targeted insecticides if Economic Threshold Level (ETL) is crossed
Provide life-saving irrigation during peak boll development

stages.title: Boll Opening & Maturation
substeps: Stop irrigation completely to allow bolls to crack open and fluff
Ensure leaves begin to senesce (dry and fall off naturally)
Protect the field from unseasonal rains if possible

stages.title: Harvesting (Picking)
substeps: Pick mature, fully opened bolls manually in the morning to retain moisture
Conduct 3-4 pickings at 10-15 day intervals
Dry the picked seed cotton in the shade before storage/ginning

stages.title: Land Preparation
substeps: Plough the land 3-4 times to a very fine tilth
Prepare raised beds or flat beds depending on soil drainage
Incorporate a heavy dose of well-rotted FYM and basal NPK

stages.title: Clove Treatment & Planting
substeps: Separate healthy, plump cloves from the main bulb
Treat cloves with Trichoderma or Carbendazim to prevent rot
Dibble cloves keeping the growing tip upwards at 15x10 cm spacing

stages.title: Early Growth & Weeding
substeps: Apply pre-emergence weedicide like Pendimethalin or Oxyfluorfen
Perform shallow hand hoeing to keep the field weed-free
Ensure soil is not disturbed deeply to protect the shallow roots

stages.title: Fertilizer & Irrigation
substeps: Top dress with Nitrogen at 30 and 45 days after planting
Apply Sulphur, which is crucial for garlic's pungency and aroma
Provide frequent, light irrigations (every 7-10 days)

stages.title: Bulb Initiation & Pest Control
substeps: Stop Nitrogen application to encourage bulb enlargement rather than leaf growth
Monitor strictly for Thrips (causes leaf curling and silvery patches)
Apply prophylactic sprays for Purple Blotch disease during high humidity

stages.title: Bulb Development & Maturation
substeps: Ensure steady moisture to prevent bulb splitting
Withhold irrigation 10-15 days before the expected harvest date
Wait until the lower leaves turn yellow and dry up

stages.title: Harvesting
substeps: Pull out the bulbs manually or loosen the soil with a fork
Harvest carefully to avoid bruising or cutting the bulbs
Tie the harvested garlic with leaves intact into small bundles

stages.title: Curing & Storage
substeps: Cure the bundles in the shade for 5-7 days to dry the outer skins
Trim the roots and cut the tops leaving 2-3 cm neck (if not storing braided)
Store in a well-ventilated, dry room to prevent sprouting or rotting

stages.title: Land Preparation
substeps: Perform deep ploughing to loosen the soil extensively
Prepare raised beds of 1-meter width and 15 cm height
Ensure excellent drainage channels between beds to prevent waterlogging

stages.title: Rhizome Treatment & Planting
substeps: Select healthy seed rhizomes (25-50g) with at least 1-2 viable buds
Treat seed rhizomes with Trichoderma and Mancozeb to prevent soft rot
Plant rhizomes in shallow pits at 25x25 cm spacing and cover with soil

stages.title: Mulching
substeps: Apply a thick layer of green leaf mulch immediately after planting
Mulching conserves moisture, prevents weed growth, and protects buds from heavy rain
Provide light irrigation to settle the soil and mulch

stages.title: Sprouting & Early Weeding
substeps: Ginger takes 3-4 weeks for complete sprouting
Perform gentle manual weeding by pulling weeds through the mulch
Ensure the beds remain moist but never waterlogged

stages.title: Fertilizer Application & Earthing Up
substeps: Apply split doses of NPK fertilizers at 45 and 90 days after planting
Perform crucial earthing up after each fertilizer application to cover the expanding rhizomes
Apply a second round of mulch (dry leaves/straw) after earthing up

stages.title: Vegetative Growth & Monsoon Management
substeps: Vigorous tillering and leafy growth occur during the monsoon
Monitor rigorously for Rhizome Rot (Soft Rot) and shoot borer
Drench the beds with 1% Bordeaux mixture as a preventive measure against rot

stages.title: Rhizome Maturation
substeps: Leaves begin to turn pale yellow, indicating maturity for green ginger
Stop irrigation completely to allow the soil to dry slightly
Ensure rhizomes are fully developed but not overly fibrous

stages.title: Harvesting (Green Ginger)
substeps: Dig out the rhizomes carefully using a spade or digging fork
Avoid cutting or bruising the delicate rhizomes
Wash thoroughly to remove soil, trim the roots, and pack for the fresh market

stages.title: Land Preparation
substeps: Perform 3-4 cross ploughings to achieve a very fine, friable tilth
Level the field perfectly to ensure uniform germination
Apply well-rotted compost and basal fertilizers (NPK)

stages.title: Seed Treatment & Sowing
substeps: Treat seeds with Carbendazim to prevent seedling blight
Line sow seeds at 20-30 cm row spacing or broadcast evenly
Sow seeds at a shallow depth (1-2 cm) and cover lightly

stages.title: Weeding & Thinning
substeps: Run a wheel hoe or rake between lines to loosen soil and remove early weeds
Perform critical thinning at 15-20 days, maintaining 5-7 cm plant-to-plant spacing
Keep the field weed-free for the first 30 days

stages.title: Fertilizer & Rapid Growth
substeps: Top dress with Urea at 3-4 weeks to support rapid vegetative growth
Ensure adequate soil moisture; jute requires high humidity and heavy rainfall
Plants grow extremely fast, reaching heights of 3-4 meters

stages.title: Pest Management
substeps: Monitor rigorously for the Jute Semilooper, Hairy Caterpillar, and Stem Weevil
Apply targeted insecticidal sprays if pest populations cross the threshold
Check for stem rot and apply preventive fungicides if water stagnation occurs

stages.title: Harvesting
substeps: Harvest between 100-120 days at the early pod formation stage for optimal fiber quality
Cut the plants close to the ground using sharp sickles
Leave the harvested plants in the field for 2-3 days to shed leaves

stages.title: Retting
substeps: Tie the defoliated stems into bundles (steeps)
Submerge the bundles in slow-moving, clean water using weights (logs or concrete blocks)
Microbiological retting loosens the fiber from the woody stick (takes 7-15 days depending on temperature)

stages.title: Fiber Extraction & Drying
substeps: Extract the fiber manually by beating the root ends and stripping the fiber from the stick
Wash the extracted fiber thoroughly in clean water to remove slimy residues
Sun-dry the fiber completely on bamboo frames for 2-3 days before baling

stages.title: Land Preparation
substeps: Conserve moisture from the preceding Kharif crop harvest
Plough the field once deeply, followed by 2 harrowings
Create a fine, firm, and level seedbed to ensure uniform germination

stages.title: Seed Treatment & Sowing
substeps: Treat seeds with Thiram or Captan to prevent seed-borne diseases
Sow seeds in lines using a seed drill at 30 cm row spacing
Maintain a sowing depth of 2-3 cm to ensure seeds contact the moisture zone

stages.title: Thinning & Early Weeding
substeps: Perform critical thinning at 15-20 days to maintain 10-12 cm intra-row spacing
Conduct one hand weeding or run a wheel hoe to aerate the soil
Apply pre-emergence herbicide like Pendimethalin if manual weeding is difficult

stages.title: Fertilizer & First Irrigation
substeps: Apply basal dose of NPK along with Sulphur (crucial for oil content)
Apply the first irrigation at the rosette stage (25-30 days after sowing)
Top dress with the remaining Nitrogen dose immediately after the first irrigation

stages.title: Flowering & Branching
substeps: The crop enters the active branching and flowering phase (yellow blooms)
Apply the second irrigation at the early pod formation stage (50-60 days)
Avoid severe moisture stress during flowering to prevent flower drop

stages.title: Pest & Disease Control
substeps: Monitor the crop rigorously for Mustard Aphids, especially on cloudy days
Spray Dimethoate or Imidacloprid if aphid population crosses the economic threshold
Check for Alternaria Blight (black spots on leaves/pods) and spray Mancozeb

stages.title: Pod Maturation
substeps: Pods swell and seeds begin to harden and accumulate oil
Leaves turn yellow and gradually drop off the plant
Stop irrigation completely to allow uniform drying of the pods

stages.title: Harvesting & Threshing
substeps: Harvest when 75-80% of the pods turn yellow to avoid heavy shattering losses
Cut the plants close to the ground in the early morning when dew prevents pods from splitting
Sun dry the harvested crop for 4-5 days, thresh manually or mechanically, and winnow

stages.title: Land Preparation & Terracing
substeps: Clear the forest or old rubber plantation
Construct terraces on hilly terrains to prevent soil erosion
Dig pits of 75x75x75 cm and fill with topsoil and rock phosphate

stages.title: Nursery & Budding
substeps: Raise seedling stumps in a nursery
Perform bud grafting (green budding or brown budding) using high-yielding clones
Maintain polybag nursery until plants are ready for transplanting

stages.title: Planting & Cover Cropping
substeps: Plant the budded stumps in the prepared pits during the onset of monsoon
Sow leguminous cover crops (like Pueraria phaseoloides) between rows
Cover cropping suppresses weeds and fixes atmospheric nitrogen

stages.title: Immature Phase Maintenance
substeps: Perform regular circle weeding around the plant base
Apply NPK fertilizers in split doses twice a year
Prune side branches up to a height of 2.5 meters to ensure a smooth trunk for tapping

stages.title: Disease Control
substeps: Monitor for Abnormal Leaf Fall (Phytophthora) during heavy monsoons
Apply prophylactic sprays of 1% Bordeaux mixture
Check for Pink Disease and treat affected bark with Bordeaux paste

stages.title: Tapping Preparation
substeps: Assess girth; trees are ready when trunk girth reaches 50 cm at 125 cm height
Mark the tapping panels at a 30-degree angle
Fix the spout and place the collection cup using a cup hanger

stages.title: Tapping (Latex Harvesting)
substeps: Excisional tapping of the bark is done early in the morning
Collect the latex in cups and transfer to buckets
Apply panel protectants to prevent Bark Rot

stages.title: Post Harvest Processing
substeps: Filter the collected latex to remove impurities
Coagulate the latex using formic acid or acetic acid
Process into Ribbed Smoked Sheets (RSS) or block rubber and dry properly

stages.title: Land Preparation
substeps: Deep plough the field to a depth of 20-25 cm
Perform harrowing and cross-ploughing to achieve a fine tilth
Prepare ridges and furrows at a spacing of 90-120 cm

stages.title: Sett Treatment & Planting
substeps: Select healthy 2-3 budded setts from 10-12 month old nursery crops
Treat setts with Carbendazim and Malathion to prevent seed-borne diseases and scale insects
Plant setts end-to-end in the furrows and cover lightly with soil

stages.title: Germination Phase
substeps: Provide light irrigation immediately after planting
Maintain optimum soil moisture to ensure uniform sprouting
Perform gap filling using pre-germinated setts if gaps are noticed

stages.title: Tillering & Earthing Up
substeps: Apply the first top dressing of Nitrogen to boost tillering (multiple shoots)
Perform partial earthing up at 45 days and full earthing up at 90-120 days
Earthing up converts furrows into ridges to prevent lodging

stages.title: Grand Growth Phase & Irrigation
substeps: This is the phase of rapid cane elongation; moisture stress must be strictly avoided
Irrigate at 10-15 day intervals
Tie the canes together (propping) to prevent lodging due to heavy winds

stages.title: Pest & Disease Control
substeps: Monitor rigorously for Early Shoot Borer and Internode Borer
Release Trichogramma chilonis egg parasitoids as a biological control
Check for Red Rot disease; uproot and destroy affected clumps

stages.title: Maturity & Ripening
substeps: Stop irrigation 15-20 days before harvest to enhance sucrose accumulation
Lower leaves dry up naturally; perform detrashes to improve aeration
Check Brix value using a hand refractometer (should be >18)

stages.title: Harvesting
substeps: Harvest the mature cane by cutting as close to the ground as possible
Remove the green tops and dry trash
Transport the cane to the sugar mill within 24 hours to prevent sucrose inversion

stages.title: Nursery Management
substeps: Select high-yielding clonal cuttings (single leaf with internode)
Plant cuttings in polythene sleeves filled with rooting medium
Maintain under shade nets and provide mist irrigation until roots establish

stages.title: Land Preparation & Shading
substeps: Clear the land and construct contour terraces on steep slopes
Dig pits of 45x45x45 cm and fill with topsoil and compost
Plant shade trees (e.g., Silver Oak) well in advance of tea planting

stages.title: Planting & Centering
substeps: Transplant 9-12 month old robust nursery plants into the pits
Perform 'centering' (cutting the main stem as close to the ground as possible) after a few months
Centering forces the plant to produce lateral branches

stages.title: Frame Formation (Pegging/Pruning)
substeps: Bend the lateral branches and peg them to the ground using wooden hooks
This encourages secondary and tertiary branching, forming a wide, flat plucking surface
Perform formative pruning to establish a strong bush frame

stages.title: Fertilizer & Weed Management
substeps: Apply NPK fertilizer mixtures specifically formulated for tea (high Nitrogen requirement)
Perform manual sickling or apply contact herbicides to manage weeds
Apply organic mulch around young plants to conserve moisture

stages.title: Pest & Disease Control
substeps: Monitor rigorously for Tea Mosquito Bug, Thrips, and Red Spider Mites
Apply Copper Oxychloride to prevent Blister Blight during the monsoon
Use integrated pest management to keep pesticide residues low

stages.title: Plucking (Harvesting)
substeps: Plucking begins when the bush frame is fully developed
Harvest 'two leaves and a bud' for high-quality tea
Pluck at intervals of 7-14 days depending on the growing season and flush

stages.title: Post Harvest Processing
substeps: Transport the green leaf to the factory immediately
Process involves Withering, Rolling (CTC or Orthodox), Fermentation (Oxidation), Drying, and Grading
Store the graded tea in moisture-proof bins before packaging

stages.title: Nursery Preparation
substeps: Prepare raised nursery beds and sterilize soil (rabbing) using dry biomass burning to control nematodes
Sow extremely tiny seeds mixed with sand for uniform distribution
Cover with straw mulch and provide fine-mist watering

stages.title: Land Prep & Transplanting
substeps: Deep plough the main field to ensure excellent drainage
Apply basal fertilizers (low Chloride, as it reduces leaf burning quality)
Transplant 6-8 week old robust seedlings at proper spacing

stages.title: Weeding & Earthing Up
substeps: Keep the field weed-free for the first 40 days
Perform inter-cultivation to aerate the soil and break the surface crust
Earth up the base of the plants to encourage root development

stages.title: Fertilizer & Irrigation
substeps: Top dress with Nitrogen carefully; excessive Nitrogen delays ripening and lowers quality
Provide light irrigation if the soil is excessively dry
Strictly avoid waterlogging, which causes 'flopping' (sudden wilting)

stages.title: Topping & Desuckering
substeps: Remove the terminal flower bud (topping) to direct plant energy into leaf expansion
Apply chemical suckericides or manually remove axillary shoots (suckers) that emerge after topping
This significantly improves leaf size, weight, and nicotine content

stages.title: Pest & Disease Control
substeps: Monitor strictly for Tobacco Caterpillar (Spodoptera) and Aphids
Check for Black Shank and Frog Eye Leaf Spot diseases
Apply recommended protective sprays avoiding residues close to harvest

stages.title: Harvesting (Priming)
substeps: Harvest leaves systematically from bottom to top as they ripen (turn yellowish-green)
This method is called 'priming' and is done in 5-6 stages
Harvested leaves are immediately tied into bunches for curing

stages.title: Curing & Grading
substeps: Flue-cure the leaves in specialized barns controlling temperature and humidity to fix the color (yellow/orange)
Alternatively, air-cure or sun-cure depending on the tobacco type (e.g., bidi or chewing tobacco)
Grade the cured leaves based on color, texture, and size, then bale them

stages.title: Land Preparation
substeps: Plough the land 3-4 times to a deep, fine tilth (crucial for rhizome expansion)
Prepare raised beds of 1-meter width and 15 cm height
Apply a heavy basal dose of well-rotted Farm Yard Manure (FYM)

stages.title: Rhizome Treatment & Planting
substeps: Select healthy mother or finger rhizomes as seed material
Treat seed rhizomes with Quinalphos and Mancozeb to prevent scale insects and rhizome rot
Plant the rhizomes in shallow pits on the beds at 25x25 cm spacing

stages.title: Mulching
substeps: Cover the planted beds entirely with green leaves or straw mulch
Mulching conserves moisture, prevents weed emergence, and protects the soil from heavy rains
Irrigate immediately to settle the mulch

stages.title: Sprouting & Weeding
substeps: Rhizomes take 3-4 weeks to sprout completely
Perform hand weeding 30-45 days after planting without disturbing the sprouting rhizomes
Maintain optimal soil moisture

stages.title: Fertilizer & Earthing Up
substeps: Apply split doses of NPK fertilizers at 45 and 90 days after planting
Perform earthing up after each fertilizer application to cover the developing rhizomes
Apply a second round of mulch after earthing up

stages.title: Vegetative Growth & Disease Control
substeps: The crop enters a phase of vigorous leafy growth
Monitor rigorously for Leaf Spot and Leaf Blotch diseases
Spray 1% Bordeaux mixture or Mancozeb to control foliar fungal diseases

stages.title: Maturation
substeps: The leaves start turning yellow and eventually dry up and fall
This indicates that the rhizomes are reaching maturity
Stop irrigation completely to allow the soil to dry and aid in harvesting

stages.title: Harvesting & Processing
substeps: Dig the field carefully to lift the clumps without cutting or bruising the rhizomes
Boil the fresh rhizomes in water until soft and emitting a characteristic odor
Sun dry the boiled rhizomes for 10-15 days until they become hard and metallic sounding, then polish
